hero.caption: 51 ft outdoor swimming pool — Stanners Hill House

blocks.content: The pool at Stanners Hill House is a 10m × 6m (51 ft) outdoor swimming pool with a capacity of 90m³. It was built in 2001 by Heritage Pools Ltd and uses a skimmer-type design. The pool house includes a 27'8 × 16'1 bar area, a 7'8 × 6'0 plant room, and shower and changing facilities.

The pool is fitted with an Eclipse automatic slatted safety cover powered by a Coverstar 230V hydraulic motor with 24V keyswitch control. Filtration is handled by a sand filter with a multiport valve.
Equipment details marked TBC will be updated once the plant room is inspected and labels are photographed.

The pool has an Eclipse automatic slatted safety cover driven by a Coverstar 230V hydraulic motor. It is operated via a 24V keyswitch — turn to CLOSE to cover the pool, turn to OPEN to uncover. The cover must be fully retracted before anyone enters the water.

blocks.title: Opening the pool cover
items: Check that nobody is in or near the pool
Turn the keyswitch to OPEN
Wait for the cover to fully retract into the housing
Confirm the cover is completely stored before swimming

blocks.title: Closing the pool cover
items: Ensure everyone is out of the pool
Remove any large debris from the water surface
Turn the keyswitch to CLOSE
Wait for the cover to fully close across the pool

blocks.content: Never operate the cover with people in the pool. The cover is not a substitute for fencing or supervision. Keep the keyswitch inaccessible to children.
Water level must be maintained 10–15 cm below the coping stone. Too low and the cover sags and collects debris. Too high and the cover floats and jams in the guide rails.

blocks.title: Cleaning the cover
items: Hose down the cover surface monthly to remove debris
Every 3–6 months, wash with mild dishwashing liquid and water
Never use a pressure washer or abrasive cleaners
Flush the guide rails with a garden hose monthly to clear dirt
Apply silicone spray to guide rails every 3 months

blocks.content: No bromine. Bromine damages the Eclipse cover slats. Use chlorine-based sanitiser only.
After shock treating the pool, leave the cover open for 24 hours before closing it.
Warranty: 20-year mechanism, 7-year fabric (3 years full + 4 pro-rated), 2-year rope.

Test the water at least twice a week during swimming season (daily in heavy use). Use a DPD test kit or photometer for accurate readings. Adjust chemicals in this order: alkalinity first, then pH, then calcium hardness, then chlorine.
Never mix chemicals — especially acid and chlorine (produces toxic chlorine gas). Always add chemicals to water, never water to chemicals. Wear gloves and goggles.

The pool uses a sand filter with a multiport valve. Water is pumped through graded silica sand which traps dirt and debris. As the filter loads up, the pressure gauge rises. When it reads 8–10 psi above the clean baseline, it's time to backwash.

rows: Reverse flush dirty sand (run until water runs clear, ~2–3 min)
Bypass filter, drain to waste (for vacuuming heavy debris)
Bypass filter, return to pool (for distributing chemicals)

blocks.content: ALWAYS turn the pump OFF before changing the multiport valve position. Changing it while the pump is running will damage the spider gasket and valve mechanism.

blocks.title: Backwash procedure
items: Turn off the pump
Turn the multiport valve to BACKWASH
Turn on the pump
Run for 2–3 minutes until the sight glass runs clear
Turn off the pump
Turn the multiport valve to RINSE
Turn on the pump and run for 30–60 seconds
Turn off the pump
Turn the multiport valve back to FILTER
Turn on the pump — note the new clean pressure reading
Top up the pool water level (backwashing uses 200–500 litres)

blocks.content: Backwash roughly every 1–2 weeks in summer, or whenever the pressure gauge reads 8–10 psi above the clean starting pressure. In autumn with heavy leaf fall, you may need to backwash more often.

Sand replacement: Every 5–7 years. Signs it's needed: filter cycles getting shorter, poor water clarity despite correct chemistry, sand appearing in the pool (broken laterals). Consider upgrading to glass filter media (AFM) for finer filtration and longer life.
During swimming season (typically May–September), complete these tasks weekly. In peak summer with heavy use, test water chemistry 2–3 times per week.

blocks.title: Weekly checklist
items: Test water chemistry (pH, free chlorine, alkalinity)
Skim the surface with a leaf net
Brush walls, floor, steps and waterline
Vacuum the pool floor
Clean the pump strainer basket
Check water level is at mid-skimmer height
Backwash the filter if pressure is up
Empty skimmer baskets
Check pump is running normally (no unusual noise)

blocks.content: The pool follows a seasonal cycle: open in spring, maintain through summer, winterise in autumn, and check through winter.

blocks.title: Spring opening (April)
items: Remove winter cover — clean, dry, fold and store it
Inspect the pool for cracks, staining, or damage
Remove any freeze plugs from skimmers and return jets
Refill pool to mid-skimmer level
Reinstall drain plugs on pump, filter and heater
Check pump lid O-ring — replace if cracked
Fill strainer pot with water to assist priming
Set multiport valve to FILTER, open all valves
Switch on the pump — check for prime and leaks
Backwash the filter for 3–5 minutes, then rinse
Test water chemistry and adjust (alkalinity → pH → calcium → chlorine)
Shock treat the pool (raise free chlorine to 10–20 ppm)
Run the pump 24 hours/day for the first 3–5 days
Brush and vacuum all surfaces
Wait at least 2 weeks before swimming — allow chemistry to stabilise

blocks.title: Autumn winterising (October)
items: Wait until water temperature drops below 15°C
Shock dose the pool and adjust pH to 7.2–7.4
Add a long-life algaecide
Lower water level 30 cm below the skimmers
Backwash the filter thoroughly
Drain all equipment (pump, filter, heater) or ensure frost thermostat is active
Fit freeze plugs (Gizzmos) into skimmers
Cover the pool with a winter debris cover
If keeping power on: confirm frost thermostat is active (triggers pump at 3–4°C)

blocks.content: Never fully drain the pool. The shell relies on the weight of water to resist hydrostatic uplift from groundwater. Surrey's clay soils have high water tables — an empty pool can crack or float out of the ground.
Mid-winter check: Falcon Pools offer a visit (£68 + VAT) to test electrics, frost thermostat, pump, chemistry, backwash filter, and check the cover pit is dry.

rows: Low chlorine, high pH, or dirty filter
Shock dose, lower pH to 7.2, backwash filter
Brush all surfaces, shock to 20 ppm, run pump 24/7, backwash repeatedly
Shock dose to 10× the combined chlorine reading
Air leak on suction side, low water level
Check pump lid O-ring, top up water to mid-skimmer
Call pool engineer — do not run a noisy pump
Sand needs replacing or valve leaking
Try extended backwash. If no improvement, call engineer
Obstruction, wrong water level, torque limiter
Check for debris in rails, check water level 10–15 cm below coping
Cover motor hums but won't move
Do not force — call cover service engineer
Do the bucket test: fill a bucket on the step, compare levels after 24 hours
Lower pH to 7.2, add scale inhibitor, check calcium hardness

blocks.content: Current maintenance contractor details to be added once confirmed.
